title: Initial Evaluation of Adult Hyponatremia
clinical_question: In adults with newly identified hyponatremia, what bedside-first evaluation approach best distinguishes urgent causes and guides initial management?
bottom_line: Confirm true hypotonic hyponatremia before expanding the differential.
Identify neurologic severity and time course early because they change urgency.
Use volume status, urine osmolality, and urine sodium to structure the first-pass evaluation.
Medication review and recent fluid exposure are high-yield and often underused.
Avoid rapid overcorrection, especially in chronic or high-risk patients.
why_this_matters: Hyponatremia is common on admission and can represent anything from excess free water intake to life-threatening intracranial or endocrine disease.
scope_and_intended_use: Resident-facing bedside synthesis for adults. Not a comprehensive guideline and not a dosing protocol.
key_definitions: True hypotonic hyponatremia: low serum sodium with low serum tonicity.
Severe symptoms: seizures, coma, or major neurologic deterioration.
Chronic hyponatremia: generally >48 hours or unknown duration.
search_strategy: PubMed: hyponatremia initial evaluation adult systematic review
Europe PMC: hyponatremia diagnostic approach adults
Guideline search: hyponatremia guideline adult diagnosis

excluded_or_deprioritized_evidence.citation_or_query: Older narrative reviews without clear methods

pathophysiology_background: The immediate issue is excess body water relative to exchangeable sodium and potassium. Etiologies vary substantially, so distinguishing tonicity and likely mechanism is the first step.
diagnosis_assessment: Start with symptom severity, measured serum osmolality, medication review, glucose, urine osmolality, and urine sodium. Interpret labs in the context of recent fluids and diuretics.
management_principles: Prioritize symptomatic stabilization, identify urgent endocrine or neurologic causes, and tailor free-water restriction, volume repletion, or cause-specific therapy to the likely mechanism.
harms_pitfalls: The major pitfall is overcorrection, especially in chronic hyponatremia or patients at high risk for osmotic demyelination.
what_remains_uncertain: Some threshold-based bedside distinctions perform inconsistently across settings, especially when patients have mixed etiologies or recent interventions.
practical_takeaways_for_residents: Confirm tonicity before labeling the mechanism.
Review medications and IV fluids early.
Escalate quickly when neurologic symptoms are present.
Be cautious with correction speed in chronic cases.
